483	\section{Affine acyclicity of the {\v C}ech complex}
484	
485	\begin{definition}
486	[Standard affine cover from a spanning family]
487	  \label{def:standard_affine_cover}
488	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.affineOpenCoverOfSpanRangeEqTop}
489	  \mathlibok
490	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib (\texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Cover.Open}).}
491	  Let \(R\) be a commutative ring (so \(R = \Gamma(\operatorname{Spec} R,
492	  \mathcal{O})\)) and let \(s : \iota \to R\) be a family of elements that generate
493	  the unit ideal, \(\operatorname{Ideal.span}(\operatorname{range} s) = \top\). The
494	  \emph{standard affine open cover} of \(\operatorname{Spec} R\) associated with
495	  \(s\) is the affine open cover whose index set is \(\iota\), whose \(i\)-th member
496	  is the basic open \(D(s_i)\), and whose \(i\)-th structure morphism is the map of
497	  affine schemes induced by the localisation homomorphism \(R \to R_{s_i}\); that
498	  is, in Mathlib notation the defining equation
499	  \(\bigl(\text{affineOpenCoverOfSpanRangeEqTop}\bigr)_f\, i =
500	  \operatorname{Spec.map}\bigl(\text{algebraMap}\; R\; (\operatorname{Localization.Away}(s_i))\bigr)\),
501	  so that the sections over the \(i\)-th piece are the \emph{away} localisation
502	  \(R_{s_i} = \operatorname{Localization.Away}(s_i)\) and the geometric piece is
503	  \(D(s_i)\). This is exactly the standard open covering
504	  \(\operatorname{Spec} R = \bigcup_i D(s_i)\) by basic opens, packaged as a single
505	  cover object. It is the spanning-family bundle \((s, hs)\) that the affine
506	  {\v C}ech vanishing of Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_acyclic_affine} is stated over.
507	\end{definition}
508	
509	\subsection{The categorical--module bridge (L1)}
510	\label{sec:cech_l1_bridge}
511	
512	The affine vanishing of Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_acyclic_affine} is a statement about the
513	\emph{section} {\v C}ech complex \(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U},
514	\mathcal{F})\) of Definition~\ref{def:section_cech_complex} --- a cochain complex of
515	abelian groups --- whereas the combinatorial contracting-homotopy core operates on a
516	concrete complex of localised \(R\)-modules. The passage between the two, the
517	\emph{categorical--module bridge}, decomposes into two self-contained statements: the
518	section identification (each section group is an away localisation, with the
519	restriction maps the canonical localisation maps) and the homology-to-exactness
520	reduction (vanishing of the abstract homology is equivalent to exactness of the
521	underlying localised-module complex). These are isolated as
522	Definition~\ref{def:qcoh_sections_localized} and
523	Lemma~\ref{lem:section_cech_homology_exact} below; the assembly --- feeding each
524	positive-degree node through the local-to-global exactness criterion and closing with
525	the dependent combinatorial port --- is carried out in the proof of
526	Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_acyclic_affine}.
527	
528	\begin{definition}
529	[Quasi-coherent sections over a basic open are an away localisation]
530	  \label{def:qcoh_sections_localized}
531	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.qcohSectionsAwayLocalized}
532	  \uses{def:section_cech_complex, def:standard_affine_cover}
533	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Schemes, Lemma \texttt{lemma-spec-sheaves} (Tag 01HV),
534	  % items (4)--(5) (read from references/stacks-schemes.tex, L701--707).
535	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "(4) For every $f\in R$ we have $\Gamma(D(f), \widetilde M) = M_f$
536	  % as an $R_f$-module.
537	  % (5) Whenever $D(g) \subset D(f)$ the restriction mappings
538	  % on $\mathcal{O}_{\Spec(R)}$ and $\widetilde M$
539	  % are the maps
540	  % $R_f \to R_g$ and $M_f \to M_g$ from Lemma
541	  % \ref{lemma-standard-open}."
542	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Schemes, \texttt{lemma-spec-sheaves} (Tag 01HV),
543	  items (4)--(5).}
544	  Let \(R\) be a commutative ring, \(\mathcal{F}\) a quasi-coherent
545	  \(\mathcal{O}_{\operatorname{Spec} R}\)-module, and write
546	  \(M = \Gamma(\operatorname{Spec} R, \mathcal{F})\) for its module of global
547	  sections. For every \(g \in R\), the restriction-of-sections map
548	  \(M = \mathcal{F}(\operatorname{Spec} R) \to \mathcal{F}(D(g))\) exhibits the
549	  section group \(\mathcal{F}(D(g))\) as the away localisation \(M_g\) of \(M\); that
550	  is, this map is an \(\operatorname{IsLocalizedModule}\) for the multiplicative
551	  submonoid \(\operatorname{Submonoid.powers} g\) of powers of \(g\). Moreover, for
552	  \(D(g_2) \subseteq D(g_1)\) the restriction map
553	  \(\mathcal{F}(D(g_1)) \to \mathcal{F}(D(g_2))\) is, under these identifications, the
554	  canonical localisation map \(M_{g_1} \to M_{g_2}\) (the unique \(R\)-linear map
555	  compatible with the localisation units). Item~(4) of Tag~01HV gives the first
556	  assertion for \(\mathcal{F} = \widetilde{M}\) (\(\Gamma(D(g), \widetilde{M}) = M_g\)
557	  as an \(R_g\)-module) and item~(5) gives the second (the restriction along
558	  \(D(g) \subset D(f)\) is the localisation map \(M_f \to M_g\)).
559	
560	  The module-level input on the side of Mathlib is the development
561	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Modules.Tilde}: the comparison map
562	  \((\operatorname{tilde.toOpen} M\, (D(g))).\mathrm{hom}\) carries the instance
563	  \(\operatorname{IsLocalizedModule}(\operatorname{Submonoid.powers} g)\), and
564	  \(\operatorname{isUnit\_algebraMap\_end\_basicOpen}\) records that \(g\) acts
565	  invertibly on the sections over \(D(g)\); the restriction compatibility is
566	  \(\operatorname{tilde.toOpen\_res}\). These supply the statement \emph{verbatim} for
567	  the standard sheaf \(\widetilde{M}\). For an \emph{arbitrary} quasi-coherent
568	  \(\mathcal{F}\) the remaining step is the canonical comparison
569	  \(\mathcal{F} \cong \widetilde{\Gamma(\operatorname{Spec} R, \mathcal{F})}\), i.e.\
570	  that the counit \(\operatorname{Scheme.Modules.fromTildeΓ}\) is an isomorphism. This
571	  is \emph{not} yet available in Mathlib for a general quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{F}\):
572	  Mathlib provides it only from a \emph{global} presentation
573	  (\(\operatorname{isIso\_fromTildeΓ\_of\_presentation}\)), whereas quasi-coherence
574	  supplies only \emph{local} presentation data on a cover. Globalising the local data
575	  on the affine \(\operatorname{Spec} R\) --- the affine equivalence
576	  \(\operatorname{QCoh}(\operatorname{Spec} R) \simeq \operatorname{Mod}(R)\), Stacks
577	  Tag~01I8 --- is the one genuine gap this definition records as a build target; it is
578	  the canonical missing affine equivalence and is self-contained.
579	\end{definition}
580	
581	\begin{lemma}
582	[Section {\v C}ech homology vanishes iff the localised complex is exact]
583	  \label{lem:section_cech_homology_exact}
584	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.sectionCech_homology_exact,
585	    AlgebraicGeometry.AwayComparison.Inverts,
586	    AlgebraicGeometry.AwayComparison.Inverts.isUnit_powers,
587	    AlgebraicGeometry.AwayComparison.Inverts.mul,
588	    AlgebraicGeometry.AwayComparison.Inverts.of_dvd,
589	    AlgebraicGeometry.AwayComparison.comparison,
590	    AlgebraicGeometry.AwayComparison.comparison_apply,
591	    AlgebraicGeometry.AwayComparison.comparison_comp,
592	    AlgebraicGeometry.AwayComparison.comparison_comp_apply,
593	    AlgebraicGeometry.AwayComparison.comparison_comp_structure,
594	    AlgebraicGeometry.AwayComparison.comparison_self,
595	    AlgebraicGeometry.AwayComparison.comparison_unique,
596	    AlgebraicGeometry.CechLocalized.cechCoeff,
597	    AlgebraicGeometry.CechLocalized.cechCoeff_transport_eq_comparison,
598	    AlgebraicGeometry.CechLocalized.cechCoface,
599	    AlgebraicGeometry.CechLocalized.cechLocalized_exact,
600	    AlgebraicGeometry.CechLocalized.cechPrepend,
601	    AlgebraicGeometry.CechLocalized.cech_hcomm,
602	    AlgebraicGeometry.CechLocalized.cech_hsh,
603	    AlgebraicGeometry.CechLocalized.cech_hu,
604	    AlgebraicGeometry.CechLocalized.sprod,
605	    AlgebraicGeometry.CechLocalized.sprod_cons,
606	    AlgebraicGeometry.CechLocalized.sprod_succAbove_dvd}
607	  \uses{def:section_cech_complex, def:qcoh_sections_localized, lem:section_cech_module_exact}
608	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, Lemma
609	  % \texttt{lemma-cech-cohomology-quasi-coherent-trivial} (the {\v C}ech complex is
610	  % the complex of localisations) (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex,
611	  % L58--67).
612	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Clearly the {\v C}ech complex
613	  % $\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F})$
614	  % is identified with the complex
615	  % $$
616	  % \prod\nolimits_{i_0} M_{f_{i_0}} \to
617	  % \prod\nolimits_{i_0i_1} M_{f_{i_0}f_{i_1}} \to
618	  % \prod\nolimits_{i_0i_1i_2} M_{f_{i_0}f_{i_1}f_{i_2}} \to
619	  % \ldots
620	  % $$"
621	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes,
622	  \texttt{lemma-cech-cohomology-quasi-coherent-trivial} (identification with the
623	  complex of localisations).}
624	  Let \(\mathcal{F}\) be a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_{\operatorname{Spec}
625	  R}\)-module and \(\mathcal{U}\) the standard cover associated with a spanning family
626	  \(s : \iota \to R\). Under the section identification of
627	  Definition~\ref{def:qcoh_sections_localized}, the section {\v C}ech complex
628	  \(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F})\) of
629	  Definition~\ref{def:section_cech_complex} is isomorphic, as a cochain complex, to
630	  the concrete complex of localised \(R\)-modules
631	  \[
632	    D^\bullet : \quad
633	    \prod_{\sigma : \{0\} \to \iota} M_{s_\sigma} \;\longrightarrow\;
634	    \prod_{\sigma : \{0,1\} \to \iota} M_{s_\sigma} \;\longrightarrow\;
635	    \cdots,
636	    \qquad
637	    s_\sigma = \prod_{k} s_{\sigma(k)},
638	  \]
639	  whose differential is the alternating sum of the canonical localisation maps. For
640	  each \(p\), vanishing of the abstract homology is equivalent to exactness of the
641	  underlying \(R\)-module maps,
642	  \[
643	    \operatorname{IsZero}\bigl(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U},
644	      \mathcal{F}).\mathrm{homology}\, p\bigr)
645	    \;\Longleftrightarrow\;
646	    \operatorname{Function.Exact}\bigl(D^{p-1} \to D^p \to D^{p+1}\bigr).
647	  \]
648	  The two directions are supplied by Mathlib: \(\operatorname{IsZero}\) of the
649	  homology is \(\operatorname{ExactAt}\) at \(p\)
650	  (\(\operatorname{exactAt\_iff\_isZero\_homology}\)), and \(\operatorname{ExactAt}\)
651	  of a short complex of \(R\)-modules is \(\operatorname{Function.Exact}\) of the
652	  underlying linear maps (\(\operatorname{ShortComplex.moduleCat\_exact\_iff}\)).
653	\end{lemma}
654	
655	\begin{proof}
656	  \uses{def:qcoh_sections_localized, def:section_cech_complex}
657	  The term identification is the degreewise statement of
658	  Definition~\ref{def:qcoh_sections_localized}: the degree-\(p\) section group
659	  \(\prod_\sigma \mathcal{F}(D(s_\sigma))\) is \(\prod_\sigma M_{s_\sigma}\), since
660	  each \((p+1)\)-fold intersection \(D(s_{\sigma(0)}) \cap \cdots \cap
661	  D(s_{\sigma(p)})\) is the basic open \(D(s_\sigma)\) of the product
662	  \(s_\sigma = \prod_k s_{\sigma(k)}\) and the sections there are the away
663	  localisation \(M_{s_\sigma}\). The differential identification is item~(5) of
664	  Definition~\ref{def:qcoh_sections_localized}: each face restriction
665	  \(\mathcal{F}(D(s_{\sigma \circ d_j})) \to \mathcal{F}(D(s_\sigma))\) is the
666	  canonical localisation map \(M_{s_{\sigma \circ d_j}} \to M_{s_\sigma}\), so the
667	  alternating sum of restrictions is the alternating sum of localisation maps, the
668	  differential of \(D^\bullet\). This gives the isomorphism of cochain complexes
669	  \(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F}) \cong D^\bullet\). The
670	  homology equivalence then follows by transporting along this isomorphism and
671	  applying \(\operatorname{exactAt\_iff\_isZero\_homology}\) (\(\operatorname{IsZero}\)
672	  homology \(\Leftrightarrow\) \(\operatorname{ExactAt}\)) and
673	  \(\operatorname{ShortComplex.moduleCat\_exact\_iff}\) (\(\operatorname{ExactAt}\) of
674	  \(R\)-modules \(\Leftrightarrow\) \(\operatorname{Function.Exact}\)).
675	
676	  The identification of the abstract section complex with the concrete localised
677	  complex \(D^\bullet\) --- together with the construction of the prepend transport
678	  \(c\) and the localisation cofaces \(\delta_k\) discharging the unit, shift, and
679	  coface-commutation compatibilities \(\mathtt{hu}\)/\(\mathtt{hsh}\)/\(\mathtt{hcomm}\)
680	  of the dependent combinatorial port --- is the bulk of the bridge. The non-circular
681	  route is reflection along the localisation equivalence on its essential image (the
682	  global-sections functor \(\Gamma\) is the inverse equivalence there, so it reflects
683	  \(\operatorname{IsZero}\) of homology); it is \emph{not} the assertion that
684	  ``\(\Gamma\) preserves homology'', which would presuppose the very affine exactness
685	  being proved.
686	\end{proof}

688	\begin{definition}
689	[The un-localised section {\v C}ech module complex \(D^\bullet\)]
690	  \label{def:section_cech_module_complex}
691	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.SectionCechModule.dCoeff,
692	    AlgebraicGeometry.SectionCechModule.dCoface,
693	    AlgebraicGeometry.SectionCechModule.dDiff,

695	    AlgebraicGeometry.SectionCechModule.dToCech,

703	    AlgebraicGeometry.SectionCechModule.locDiff_eq_depDiff,
704	    AlgebraicGeometry.SectionCechModule.locDiff_exact,
705	    AlgebraicGeometry.SectionCechModule.fLoc,
706	    AlgebraicGeometry.SectionCechModule.fLoc_apply,
707	    AlgebraicGeometry.SectionCechModule.fLoc_isLocalizedModule,
708	    AlgebraicGeometry.SectionCechModule.locDiff_fLoc,
709	    AlgebraicGeometry.SectionCechModule.map_dDiff_eq_locDiff,
710	    AlgebraicGeometry.SectionCechModule.spanIdx,
711	    AlgebraicGeometry.SectionCechModule.spanIdx_spec,
712	    AlgebraicGeometry.AwayComparison.comparison_isLocalizedModule,
713	    AlgebraicGeometry.AwayComparison.Inverts.smul_pow_cancel}
714	  \uses{def:standard_affine_cover, def:qcoh_sections_localized}
715	  Let \(R\) be a commutative ring, \(M = \Gamma(\operatorname{Spec} R, \mathcal{F})\)
716	  the global sections of a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_{\operatorname{Spec}
717	  R}\)-module \(\mathcal{F}\), and \(s : \iota \to R\) (\(\iota\) finite) a spanning
718	  family of the unit ideal, \(\operatorname{Ideal.span}(\operatorname{range} s) =
719	  \top\) (Definition~\ref{def:standard_affine_cover}). The \emph{un-localised section
720	  {\v C}ech module complex} \(D^\bullet\) is the cochain complex of \(R\)-modules
721	  whose degree-\(p\) term is the product over multi-indices
722	  \(\sigma : \{0, \ldots, p\} \to \iota\) of the away localisations
723	  \[
724	    D^p \;=\; \prod_{\sigma} M_{s_\sigma},
725	    \qquad s_\sigma = \prod_{k} s_{\sigma(k)},
726	    \qquad M_{s_\sigma} = \operatorname{LocalizedModule}
727	      \bigl(\operatorname{Submonoid.powers}(s_\sigma)\bigr)\, M,
728	  \]
729	  with differential \(d^p : D^p \to D^{p+1}\) the alternating sum
730	  \(\sum_{j} (-1)^j\, \delta^j\) of the coface comparison maps \(\delta^j\), each of
731	  which is the canonical away-localisation comparison
732	  \(M_{s_{\sigma \circ d_j}} \to M_{s_\sigma}\) sending a fraction to its image under
733	  the further localisation at the omitted factor. The single-coefficient comparison
734	  map and its localisation property are recorded by the \(\mathtt{dCoface}\) and
735	  \(\mathtt{dCoeff}\) data and the differential by \(\mathtt{dDiff}\) (with action
736	  \(\mathtt{dDiff\_apply}\)); the auxiliary \(\mathtt{spanIdx}\)/\(\mathtt{spanIdx\_spec}\)
737	  select a witnessing index of the spanning relation.

739	  The complex \(D^\bullet\) is tied to the section {\v C}ech complex of
740	  Definition~\ref{def:section_cech_complex} and to its localisations by three

742	  \(D^p\) with the degree-\(p\) section group

744	  Definition~\ref{def:qcoh_sections_localized} as a localised module
745	  (\(\mathtt{dToCech\_isLocalizedModule}\)), intertwining the differentials
746	  (\(\mathtt{cechCoface\_dToCech}\), \(\mathtt{dToCech\_comm}\)); the section-side

749	  \emph{localised} {\v C}ech complex: the localised differential \(\mathtt{locDiff}\)
750	  (action \(\mathtt{locDiff\_apply}\)) agrees, via
751	  \(\mathtt{locDiff\_eq\_depDiff}\), with the dependent-coefficient combinatorial

755	  (\(\mathtt{fLoc\_isLocalizedModule}\)) intertwining \(\mathtt{dDiff}\) with
756	  \(\mathtt{locDiff}\) (\(\mathtt{locDiff\_fLoc}\), \(\mathtt{map\_dDiff\_eq\_locDiff}\)).
757	  The key algebraic fact underlying all three families is the localisation
758	  transitivity \(M_a[1/b] = M_{ab}\): localising the away module \(M_{s_\sigma}\)

760	  \(\mathtt{AwayComparison.comparison\_isLocalizedModule}\) (the composite of two away

762	  bookkeeping \(\mathtt{AwayComparison.Inverts.smul\_pow\_cancel}\). Thus localising
763	  \(D^\bullet\) at \(s_r\) (an \(\operatorname{IsLocalizedModule.Away}\)) reproduces

961	  The homotopy is built directly from the away-localisation identification, \emph{not}

967	  is used instead.
968	\end{proof}
969	
970	\subsection{Presheaf-level {\v C}ech machinery}
971	\label{sec:presheaf_cech_machinery}
972	
973	The {\v C}ech-acyclicity of injective \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules
974	(Lemma~\ref{lem:injective_cech_acyclic} below) is not a statement about a single
975	affine cover: it is proved on the abelian category \(\mathrm{PMod}(\mathcal{O}_X)\)
976	of presheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules, where the {\v C}ech complex of sections
977	is the \(\operatorname{Hom}\)-complex of an explicit free resolution. The argument
978	rests on two presheaf-level facts, recorded here, and one transfer of injectivity.
979	First, a free complex \(K(\mathcal{U})_\bullet\) of presheaves of modules whose
980	\(\operatorname{Hom}\)-complex into \(\mathcal{F}\) \emph{is} the {\v C}ech complex
981	of sections (Definition~\ref{def:cech_free_presheaf_complex},
982	Definition~\ref{def:section_cech_complex} and
983	Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_complex_hom_identification}). Second, the resolution property of
984	that complex: \(K(\mathcal{U})_\bullet\) resolves \(\mathcal{O}_{\mathcal{U}}\)
985	concentrated in degree \(0\) (Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_free_complex_quasi_iso}). Together
986	these give: an injective \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module is injective as a presheaf
987	(Lemma~\ref{lem:injective_cech_acyclic} via
988	Lemma~\ref{lem:injective_of_adjoint} applied to the sheafification adjunction
989	Lemma~\ref{lem:mod_pmod_adjunction}), so \(\operatorname{Hom}(-, \mathcal{I})\) is
990	exact and carries the augmented resolution to an exact section complex, i.e.\
991	\(\check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{I}) = 0\) for \(p > 0\). This direct route uses
992	neither enough-injectives in \(\mathrm{PMod}(\mathcal{O}_X)\) nor a right-derived
993	\(\delta\)-functor identification.
994	
995	\begin{definition}
996	
997	[Free presheaf complex of a cover]
998	  \label{def:cech_free_presheaf_complex}
999	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.cechFreePresheafComplex,
1000	    AlgebraicGeometry.freeYoneda,
1001	    AlgebraicGeometry.coverOpen,
1002	    AlgebraicGeometry.coverInterOpen,
1003	    AlgebraicGeometry.coverInterOpen_comp_le,
1004	    AlgebraicGeometry.cechFreeSimplicial,
1005	    AlgebraicGeometry.cechFreePresheafComplex_X,
1006	    AlgebraicGeometry.sigma_ι_eqToHom_transport}
1007	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology, Lemma \texttt{lemma-cech-map-into}
1008	  % (the complex $K(\mathcal{U})_\bullet$) (read from
1009	  % references/stacks-cohomology.tex, L1142--1162, with the $j_{p!}$ description
1010	  % L1095--1135).
1011	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Consider the chain complex $K(\mathcal{U})_\bullet$
1012	  % of presheaves of $\mathcal{O}_X$-modules
1013	  % $$
1014	  % \ldots
1015	  % \to
1016	  % \bigoplus_{i_0i_1i_2} (j_{i_0i_1i_2})_{p!}\mathcal{O}_{U_{i_0i_1i_2}}
1017	  % \to
1018	  % \bigoplus_{i_0i_1} (j_{i_0i_1})_{p!}\mathcal{O}_{U_{i_0i_1}}
1019	  % \to
1020	  % \bigoplus_{i_0} (j_{i_0})_{p!}\mathcal{O}_{U_{i_0}}
1021	  % \to 0 \to \ldots
1022	  % $$
1023	  % where the last nonzero term is placed in degree $0$
1024	  % and where the map
1025	  % $$
1026	  % (j_{i_0\ldots i_{p + 1}})_{p!}\mathcal{O}_{U_{i_0\ldots i_{p + 1}}}
1027	  % \longrightarrow
1028	  % (j_{i_0\ldots \hat i_j \ldots i_{p + 1}})_{p!}
1029	  % \mathcal{O}_{U_{i_0\ldots \hat i_j \ldots i_{p + 1}}}
1030	  % $$
1031	  % is given by $(-1)^j$ times the canonical map."
1032	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology,
1033	  \texttt{lemma-cech-map-into} (the free complex \(K(\mathcal{U})_\bullet\)).}
1034	  Let \(X\) be a ringed space and \(\mathcal{U} : U = \bigcup_{i \in I} U_i\) an
1035	  open covering, with \(j_{i_0 \ldots i_p} : U_{i_0 \ldots i_p} \hookrightarrow X\)
1036	  the open immersion of each finite intersection. The Stacks formulation uses, for
1037	  each intersection \(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\), the \emph{extension-by-zero} presheaf
1038	  \((j_{i_0 \ldots i_p})_{!}\,\mathcal{O}_X|_{U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}}\), the presheaf of
1039	  modules sending an open \(W\) to \(\mathcal{O}_X(W)\) when
1040	  \(W \subset U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\) and to \(0\) otherwise. This extension-by-zero
1041	  presheaf is, canonically, the \emph{free presheaf of modules on the representable}
1042	  \(\operatorname{free}(\mathbf{y}\,U_{i_0 \ldots i_p})\): writing
1043	  \(\mathbf{y}\,V = \operatorname{Hom}(-, V)\) for the Yoneda presheaf of the open
1044	  \(V\) on the site of opens of \(X\), the free presheaf of modules
1045	  \(\operatorname{free}(\mathbf{y}\,V)\) sends \(W\) to the free
1046	  \(\mathcal{O}_X(W)\)-module on the set \(\operatorname{Hom}(W, V)\), which is
1047	  \(\mathcal{O}_X(W)\) if \(W \subset V\) and \(0\) otherwise — exactly
1048	  extension-by-zero of \(\mathcal{O}_X|_V\). Its defining universal property
1049	  \[
1050	    \operatorname{Hom}_{\mathrm{PMod}(\mathcal{O}_X)}\bigl(
1051	      \operatorname{free}(\mathbf{y}\,V),\, \mathcal{F}\bigr)
1052	    \;\cong\; \mathcal{F}(V)
1053	  \]
1054	  is the free–forgetful adjunction for presheaves of modules composed with the
1055	  Yoneda isomorphism \(\operatorname{Hom}(\mathbf{y}\,V, G) \cong G(V)\). The
1056	  \emph{free presheaf complex} of \(\mathcal{U}\) is the chain complex
1057	  \(K(\mathcal{U})_\bullet\) in the abelian category
1058	  \(\mathrm{PMod}(\mathcal{O}_X)\) of presheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules whose
1059	  term in degree \(p\) is the direct sum
1060	  \[
1061	    K(\mathcal{U})_p
1062	      = \bigoplus_{(i_0, \ldots, i_p) \in I^{p+1}}
1063	        \operatorname{free}\bigl(\mathbf{y}\,U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\bigr),
1064	  \]
1065	  with \(K(\mathcal{U})_p\) placed in degree \(p \geq 0\) (and \(0\) in negative
1066	  degrees), and whose differential \(K(\mathcal{U})_{p+1} \to K(\mathcal{U})_p\) is
1067	  the alternating sum \(\sum_{j=0}^{p+1} (-1)^j\) of the maps
1068	  \(\operatorname{free}(\mathbf{y}\,U_{i_0 \ldots i_{p+1}}) \to
1069	  \operatorname{free}(\mathbf{y}\,U_{i_0 \ldots \widehat{i_j} \ldots i_{p+1}})\)
1070	  obtained by applying \(\operatorname{free}\) to the representable index-dropping
1071	  maps \(\mathbf{y}\,U_{i_0 \ldots i_{p+1}} \to \mathbf{y}\,U_{i_0 \ldots
1072	  \widehat{i_j} \ldots i_{p+1}}\) induced by the inclusions of opens
1073	  \(U_{i_0 \ldots i_{p+1}} \subset U_{i_0 \ldots \widehat{i_j} \ldots i_{p+1}}\)
1074	  that drop the index \(i_j\). Each summand is a \emph{projective} object of
1075	  \(\mathrm{PMod}(\mathcal{O}_X)\), because the universal property above identifies
1076	  \(\operatorname{Hom}_{\mathcal{O}_X}(\operatorname{free}(\mathbf{y}\,U_{i_0 \ldots
1077	  i_p}), -)\) with the exact ``sections over \(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\)'' functor
1078	  \((-)(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p})\); this is the structural feature exploited below.

1691	\begin{lemma}[Right adjoint of a mono-preserving left adjoint preserves injectives]

1698	  categories such that the left adjoint \(L\) preserves monomorphisms. Then for every
1699	  injective object \(J\) of the target, \(R(J)\) is an injective object of the source.

1705	  \mathlibok

1709	  sheafification is left adjoint to the inclusion
1710	  \(\mathrm{Mod}(\mathcal{O}_X) \hookrightarrow \mathrm{PMod}(\mathcal{O}_X)\) of

1721	
1722	\begin{lemma}
1723	[Injective modules are {\v C}ech-acyclic]
1724	  \label{lem:injective_cech_acyclic}
1725	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.injective_cech_acyclic,
1726	    AlgebraicGeometry.injective_toPresheafOfModules}
1727	  \uses{def:cech_complex, def:section_cech_complex, lem:cech_complex_hom_identification, lem:cech_free_complex_quasi_iso, lem:injective_of_adjoint, lem:mod_pmod_adjunction}
1728	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology, Lemma

1745	  \texttt{lemma-injective-trivial-cech}.}

1777	% quasi-isomorphic to $\mathcal{O}_\mathcal{U}[0]$. Hence by
1778	% the exactness of Hom into $\mathcal{I}$ mentioned above we see
1779	% that $\check{H}^i(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{I}) = 0$ for all
1780	% $i > 0$."
1781	\begin{proof}
1782	  \uses{def:cech_complex, def:section_cech_complex, lem:cech_complex_hom_identification, lem:cech_free_complex_quasi_iso, lem:injective_of_adjoint, lem:mod_pmod_adjunction}
1783	  The argument has two independent parts.
1784	
1785	  \emph{Part 1: an injective sheaf is injective as a presheaf.} Sheafification is
1786	  left adjoint to the inclusion
1787	  \(\iota : \mathrm{Mod}(\mathcal{O}_X) \hookrightarrow \mathrm{PMod}(\mathcal{O}_X)\)
1788	  of sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules into presheaves of
1789	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules (Lemma~\ref{lem:mod_pmod_adjunction}), and its left
1790	  adjoint sheafification is exact, hence preserves monomorphisms. By
1791	  Lemma~\ref{lem:injective_of_adjoint} the right adjoint \(\iota\) carries injective
1792	  objects to injective objects; thus an injective \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module
1793	  \(\mathcal{I}\) is injective as an object of \(\mathrm{PMod}(\mathcal{O}_X)\). In
1794	  particular the contravariant functor \(\operatorname{Hom}_{\mathcal{O}_X}(-,
1795	  \mathcal{I})\) is exact on \(\mathrm{PMod}(\mathcal{O}_X)\).
1796	
1797	  \emph{Part 2: vanishing.} By Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_free_complex_quasi_iso} the free
1798	  presheaf complex \(K(\mathcal{U})_\bullet\) of
1799	  Definition~\ref{def:cech_free_presheaf_complex} is a resolution of
1800	  \(\mathcal{O}_{\mathcal{U}}\) concentrated in degree \(0\); equivalently the
1801	  augmented complex \(\cdots \to K(\mathcal{U})_1 \to K(\mathcal{U})_0 \to
1802	  \mathcal{O}_{\mathcal{U}} \to 0\) is exact in \(\mathrm{PMod}(\mathcal{O}_X)\).
1803	  Applying the exact functor \(\operatorname{Hom}_{\mathcal{O}_X}(-, \mathcal{I})\)
1804	  of Part 1 to this exact augmented complex yields an exact complex; by the
1805	  identification \(\operatorname{Hom}_{\mathcal{O}_X}(K(\mathcal{U})_\bullet,
1806	  \mathcal{I}) = \check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{I})\) of
1807	  Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_complex_hom_identification} (with the section {\v C}ech complex
1808	  of Definition~\ref{def:section_cech_complex}), this is precisely the statement that
1809	  the section {\v C}ech complex of \(\mathcal{I}\) is exact in positive degrees, with
1810	  \(\operatorname{Hom}_{\mathcal{O}_X}(\mathcal{O}_{\mathcal{U}}, \mathcal{I}) =
1811	  \mathcal{I}(U)\) supplying degree \(0\). Hence
1812	  \(\check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{I}) = 0\) for all \(p > 0\) and
1813	  \(\check{H}^0(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{I}) = \mathcal{I}(U)\). This direct route uses
1814	  neither enough-injectives in \(\mathrm{PMod}(\mathcal{O}_X)\) nor a right-derived
1815	  \(\delta\)-functor identification.
1816	\end{proof}
1817

1276	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology, \texttt{lemma-homology-complex}.}
1277	  Let \(X\) be a ringed space and \(\mathcal{U} : U = \bigcup_{i \in I} U_i\) an open
1278	  covering. The \emph{cover structure presheaf} \(\mathcal{O}_{\mathcal{U}} :
1279	  X.\mathrm{PresheafOfModules}\) is the image presheaf of the augmentation
1280	  \[
1281	    \coprod_i \operatorname{free}(\mathbf{y}\,U_i) \;\longrightarrow\; \mathbf{1},
1282	  \]
1283	  where the target \(\mathbf{1} = \operatorname{PresheafOfModules.unit}(
1284	  X.\mathrm{ringCatSheaf}.\mathrm{obj})\) is the structure presheaf \(\mathcal{O}_X\)
1285	  regarded as a presheaf of modules over itself. Concretely
1286	  \(\mathcal{O}_{\mathcal{U}}(W) = \mathcal{O}_X(W)\) when \(W\) is contained in some
1287	  \(U_i\), and \(0\) otherwise. The degree-\(0\) term
1288	  \(K(\mathcal{U})_0 = \coprod_i \operatorname{free}(\mathbf{y}\,U_i)\) of the free
1289	  presheaf complex of Definition~\ref{def:cech_free_presheaf_complex} carries the
1290	  augmentation map \(K(\mathcal{U})_0 \to \mathcal{O}_{\mathcal{U}}\) onto this image
1291	  presheaf.

1294	\begin{lemma}
1295	[Objectwise detection of quasi-isomorphisms of presheaf-of-module complexes]
1296	  \label{lem:quasiIso_of_evaluation}

1300	  \uses{def:cech_free_presheaf_complex}

1303	  % references/stacks-cohomology.tex, L1220--1226).

1305	  % complex over any open $W \subset X$ leads to an acyclic complex."
1306	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology, \texttt{lemma-homology-complex}
1307	  (sectionwise reduction).}
1308	  Let \(R\) be a presheaf of rings on a category \(C\) and let
1309	  \(\varphi : K \to L\) be a morphism of chain complexes in the abelian category
1310	  \(\mathrm{PMod}(R)\) of presheaves of \(R\)-modules. Suppose that for \emph{every}
1311	  object \(V\) the evaluated morphism — the image of \(\varphi\) under the functor
1312	  \(\bigl(\operatorname{PresheafOfModules.evaluation} R\, V\bigr).
1313	  \operatorname{mapHomologicalComplex}\), a morphism of complexes of
1314	  \(R(V)\)-modules — is a quasi-isomorphism. Then \(\varphi\) is a
1315	  quasi-isomorphism. This is the objectwise-reduction step that turns the
1316	  resolution claim of Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_free_complex_quasi_iso} into a family of
1317	  sectionwise (combinatorial) statements: homology in \(\mathrm{PMod}(R)\) is
1318	  computed open-by-open, because the evaluation functors
1319	  \(\operatorname{PresheafOfModules.evaluation} R\, V\) are exact (hence preserve
1320	  homology) and jointly conservative. The Lean entry point is
1321	  \(\operatorname{quasiIso\_of\_evaluation}\), which combines the joint
1322	  conservativity of the evaluations (each \(g\) is an isomorphism as soon as every
1323	  \((\operatorname{evaluation} R\, V).\operatorname{map} g\) is) with the fact that
1324	  each \(\operatorname{PresheafOfModules.evaluation} R\, V\) preserves homology.
1325	\end{lemma}
1326	
1327	\begin{proof}
1328	  \uses{def:cech_free_presheaf_complex}
1329	  A morphism of complexes is a quasi-isomorphism iff it induces an isomorphism on
1330	  homology in every degree (\(\operatorname{quasiIso\_iff}\),
1331	  \(\operatorname{quasiIsoAt\_iff\_isIso\_homologyMap}\)). Fix a degree \(i\). The
1332	  homology map \(H_i(\varphi)\) is an isomorphism in \(\mathrm{PMod}(R)\) iff each of
1333	  its evaluations is an isomorphism, since the underlying
1334	  presheaf-of-abelian-groups functor reflects isomorphisms and a morphism of
1335	  presheaves is an isomorphism iff it is so objectwise (joint conservativity). For
1336	  each \(V\), evaluation preserves homology, so the evaluation of \(H_i(\varphi)\)
1337	  is the homology map of the evaluated complex morphism; the latter is an
1338	  isomorphism because the evaluated morphism is a quasi-isomorphism by hypothesis.
1339	  Hence \(H_i(\varphi)\) is an isomorphism for all \(i\), i.e.\ \(\varphi\) is a
1340	  quasi-isomorphism.
1341	\end{proof}

1358	  (description of the evaluated complex).}

/-! ## Project-local Mathlib supplement — `Hom(-, I)` is exact for injective `I`

The presheaf-level Čech-acyclicity argument (`lem:injective_cech_acyclic`) needs the single
categorical fact that, for an injective object `I` of an abelian category, the contravariant
hom functor `Hom(-, I)` is exact — equivalently, the covariant functor
`preadditiveYoneda.obj I : Cᵒᵖ ⥤ Ab` preserves homology. Mathlib supplies the two pieces:
`preadditiveYoneda.obj I` always preserves finite limits (it is left exact), and injectivity
is equivalent to it preserving epimorphisms; we assemble these into preservation of finite
colimits (hence of homology), and feed the result through
`HomologicalComplex.quasiIsoAt_map_of_preservesHomology` to obtain quasi-isomorphism
preservation. These are stated for a general abelian category so they apply verbatim to
`X.PresheafOfModules` once the injectivity of `I` as a presheaf of modules is in hand.

Project-local: Mathlib has the ingredients but not the packaged exactness of `Hom(-, I)` as a
homology-preserving functor. -/

section InjectiveHomExact

open CategoryTheory Limits

variable {C : Type*} [Category C] [Abelian C]

/-- **`Hom(-, I)` preserves finite colimits for injective `I`.** For an injective object `I`
of an abelian category, the covariant functor `preadditiveYoneda.obj I : Cᵒᵖ ⥤ Ab`
preserves finite colimits: it is left exact (preserves finite limits) automatically, and
injectivity makes it preserve epimorphisms, which together with left exactness gives
exactness. Combined with the automatic `PreservesFiniteLimits` instance this yields
`(preadditiveYoneda.obj I).PreservesHomology` by `Functor.preservesHomologyOfExact`.

Project-local: the exactness of `Hom(-, I)` for injective `I` is the categorical engine of
`lem:injective_cech_acyclic`. -/
instance preadditiveYoneda_obj_preservesFiniteColimits_of_injective
    (I : C) [Injective I] : PreservesFiniteColimits (preadditiveYoneda.obj I) := by
  have hepi : (preadditiveYoneda.obj I).PreservesEpimorphisms :=
    (Injective.injective_iff_preservesEpimorphisms_preadditiveYoneda_obj I).mp inferInstance
  rw [Functor.preservesFiniteColimits_iff_forall_exact_map_and_epi]
  intro S hS
  have hg := hS.epi_g
  exact ⟨hS.exact.map_of_mono_of_preservesKernel _ hS.mono_f inferInstance,
    hepi.preserves S.g⟩

/-- **`Hom(-, I)` carries quasi-isomorphisms to quasi-isomorphisms for injective `I`.**
Since `preadditiveYoneda.obj I` preserves homology
(`preadditiveYoneda_obj_preservesFiniteColimits_of_injective`), applying it degreewise to a
quasi-isomorphism `φ` of chain complexes in `Cᵒᵖ` produces a quasi-isomorphism of the
`Hom`-cochain complexes. This is the form consumed by `injective_cech_acyclic`: the
augmentation `cechFreeComplexAug 𝒰` (a quasi-isomorphism once Lane 1 lands), taken opposite
and mapped through `preadditiveYoneda.obj F`, stays a quasi-isomorphism.

Project-local: packages `quasiIsoAt_map_of_preservesHomology` against the injective-hom
exactness instance above. -/
lemma quasiIso_map_preadditiveYoneda_of_injective (I : C) [Injective I]
    {ι : Type*} {c : ComplexShape ι} {K L : HomologicalComplex Cᵒᵖ c}
    (φ : K ⟶ L) [QuasiIso φ] :
    QuasiIso (((preadditiveYoneda.obj I).mapHomologicalComplex c).map φ) := by
  rw [quasiIso_iff]
  intro i
  exact HomologicalComplex.quasiIsoAt_map_of_preservesHomology φ (preadditiveYoneda.obj I) i
    (hφ := QuasiIso.quasiIsoAt (f := φ) i)

end InjectiveHomExact

end AlgebraicGeometry

- Read the relevant blueprint chapter. It often contains the mathematical path to the ingredient you're building.

### 2. Scout the Mathlib API

Before writing any Lean, search for what already exists:

- `lean_local_search` for related names.
- `lean_leansearch` with a description of the mathematical content.
- `lean_loogle` for simple type patterns.
- Look for near-misses: a lemma that covers a sub-case, an instance you can compose, a `simp` set that contains what you need.

### 3. Build bottom-up, step by step

Start from the deepest missing piece identified in your scouting. For each step:

1. **Draft the type signature** and check it typechecks via `lean_diagnostic_messages` or `lean_run_code` before attempting the body.

4. **Continue to the next step** in the chain.

Push as far as you can. Each axiom-clean step you add is permanent progress.

Before declaring a step impossible:

1. **Try a different proof route.** Reformulate the statement, weaken to a special case you can prove, use a detour through an equivalent form.
2. **Use the informal agent** if an API key is available (check `env | grep -E "DEEPSEEK|MOONSHOT|OPENROUTER|OPENAI|GEMINI"` first): `.claude/tools/archon-informal-agent.py` "Prove [goal] using only current Mathlib." Formalize whatever it suggests. If no key is set, skip to step 3.

4. **Prove a strictly smaller sub-step** that is axiom-clean and genuinely useful — something that shrinks the remaining gap.

Only after exhausting these alternatives do you stop. **Comments describing an approach you haven't tried are not exhausted alternatives — attempt them first.**

**Before stopping, run this self-review:**

1. Did I attempt every approach I wrote in a comment, TODO, or log entry? If not, go back and attempt them.

One section per declaration attempted. For each: approach, result (RESOLVED / FAILED / PARTIAL / NOT ADDED), dead-end warnings, next step.

## End-of-session handoff

**Write a `## Summary` section** before the verdicts: declarations added (count + names), declarations blocked (count + why), sorry count before → after across your file.

**Write a `## Needs blueprint entry` section.** This mode is the biggest source of blueprint debt: every non-private definition/lemma you add is, by construction, infrastructure with **no blueprint block yet** — a `lean_aux` node invisible to the dependency graph (`archon dag-query unmatched` will list it) until the planner/reviewer blueprints it. List each new non-private declaration (name, file, and the facts its proof relies on) so the 1-to-1 Lean ↔ blueprint correspondence the dag agent built is restored next iter. Closing 0 project sorries while adding 3 unreported helpers does not advance the project — it quietly re-introduces the isolation the DAG phase worked to eliminate. Reporting them is mandatory.

**Write a `## Why I stopped` section** — be brutally honest. Only axiom-clean Lean declarations count as progress. Comments, sketches, and log prose do NOT. If you wrote down approaches you didn't attempt, say so:

- `Real progress`: N axiom-clean declarations added — name each one with its line number.
- `Partial progress`: added some declarations, hit a specific blocker on the next — name the blocker precisely (not "it's complex", but the exact type or missing ingredient).
- `Approaches written but not attempted`: identified routes in comments or log but did not attempt them — name each. This is valid but weak; the planner will re-assign with a directive to attempt them.
- `Blocked — alternatives exhausted`: informal agent called (or key unavailable, documented), alternative routes tried, all failed — name what was tried, why each failed, and the exact type statement of the next needed ingredient.
- `Infrastructure already exists`: Mathlib has what was needed — cite the exact lemma name and namespace.

content: I'll start by orienting myself with the project files.

1	# Archon Project
2	
3	You are one of: the plan agent, a prover agent, a subagent (per descriptor in `.archon/subagents/`), or the review agent. Read `PROGRESS.md` to determine your role and current objectives. Keep workspace tidy. Prefer existing MCP tools.
4	
5	## Priority Rule
6	
7	When instructions conflict between global and local sources, **local takes precedence**:
8	
9	- Prompts in `.archon/prompts/` override Archon's global prompts.
10	- Skills in `.claude/skills/` override globally installed plugins.
11	- Rules in `.claude/rules/` apply only to this project.
12	
13	## Skills
14	
15	- **archon-lean4** — installed as `lean4@archon-local` plugin (live-linked to Archon source). Provides `/archon-lean4:prove`, `/archon-lean4:golf`, `/archon-lean4:doctor`, etc.
16	
17	## Tools
18	
19	Project tools live in `.claude/tools/` as directly-executable scripts. List with `ls .claude/tools/`; run `<path> --help`. MCP servers (Lean LSP, etc.) are registered in `.claude/settings.json` / `.mcp.json` and surface as `mcp__*` tools.
20	
21	Two always-present scripts:
22	
23	- **`archon-informal-agent.py`** — call external LLMs for an informal proof-style sketch when you're stuck on an approach and want a second opinion. Supports DeepSeek, Kimi (OpenAI-compatible via `--provider kimi`), Kimi (Anthropic-compatible via `--provider kimi-anthropic`), OpenRouter, OpenAI, and Gemini; defaults to `--provider auto` which picks the best available key automatically. **Requires at least one key in env** (`DEEPSEEK_API_KEY` / `MOONSHOT_API_KEY` / `OPENROUTER_API_KEY` / `OPENAI_API_KEY` / `GEMINI_API_KEY`) — check via `env | grep -E "DEEPSEEK|MOONSHOT|OPENROUTER|OPENAI|GEMINI"` BEFORE planning around its use; if no key is set, fall back to alternatives below. Both `kimi` and `kimi-anthropic` use the same `MOONSHOT_API_KEY`; the latter speaks Moonshot's Anthropic-compatible Messages endpoint and supports `--think`. The output is LLM-generated from training data, NOT source-derived: this tool is **not** a literature retriever. For actual literature lookup (cross-check a claim against Hartshorne, fetch an arXiv paper, summarize a Stacks tag) consult the auto-injected subagent catalog — when a literature/reference-fetching subagent is enabled it will appear there — or use the `WebSearch` / `WebFetch` tools directly.
24	- **`archon-subagent.py`** — the generic subagent dispatcher (one wrapper handles every subagent — see "Subagents" below).
25	
26	## Subagents
27	
28	Subagents are descriptor files at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md` (YAML frontmatter — `name`, `description`, `write_domain`, `read_only`, `can_spawn`, `default_enabled`, optional `mandatory: [<phase>...]`, optional `dispatcher_notes` — followed by the prompt body the spawned Claude reads).
29	
30	**You do NOT need to discover subagents.** The plan and review prompts auto-inject an **Available subagents** section at the top of each invocation listing every enabled descriptor with description, write-domain, MANDATORY flag, and `dispatcher_notes`. When you decide to invoke a subagent, read its full prompt at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md`.
31	
32	**Invoke** via the generic wrapper (Bash tool, foreground):
33	
34	```
35	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
36	  --name <subagent-name> \
37	  --slug <kebab-slug> \
38	  --directive-file <path-to-directive.md> \
39	  --write-domain '<glob>'        # repeat for multiple
40	```
41	
42	The wrapper exits 0 on success, prints a one-line status, writes the report to `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`, and the Archon CLI auto-archives a copy to `logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-report.md` for the dashboard. The dispatch semaphore caps concurrent subagent processes at `loop.max_parallel`; the wrapper resolves `--parent-slug` from `ARCHON_SUBAGENT_SLUG` so nested children inherit the hierarchy.
43	
44	**Treat each dispatch as blocking — wait for its report before acting on it.** Don't *deliberately* set `run_in_background: true`. But note the wrapper is genuinely synchronous (it `subprocess.run`s the `archon subagent` CLI and only returns once the child finishes and its report is on disk), so the harness may *auto-background* a long-running dispatch and hand you a task ID immediately — that's expected and fine. When it does, await that task / poll for the report file at `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md` (the wrapper's one-line status and exit 0 confirm completion) before you read or act on the result. The thing to avoid is firing a dispatch and then continuing to plan as if it had already returned; whether the call blocked inline or was auto-backgrounded, the rule is the same: do not consume a subagent's output until its report exists.
45	
46	**Highly-recommended subagents.** A descriptor whose frontmatter sets `mandatory: [plan]` is treated as **highly recommended** for the plan phase (similarly `[review]` for the review phase). The catalog tags them `[HIGHLY RECOMMENDED]`. Dispatch each one unless you have a concrete reason to skip — when skipping, record a one-line rationale under a `## Subagent skips` section in `iter/iter-NNN/<phase>.md` (e.g. `- strategy-critic: STRATEGY.md SHA unchanged from prior iter and last verdict was SOUND`). A post-phase audit checks both signals: it silences when the subagent dispatched OR a skip rationale was recorded, and warns only when neither happened. Each subagent's `dispatcher_notes` enumerates its specific skip conditions — read them before deciding. **Subagents ship disabled by default** (`default_enabled: false`), so the rule only fires for subagents the user has explicitly listed under `subagents.enabled` in `.archon/config.json`; on a fresh project the catalog is empty and no recommended dispatch is required. (The frontmatter field is still named `mandatory: [...]` for backward compat with existing configs and descriptors — its semantic meaning is "highly recommended", not "must dispatch".)
47	
48	## Key Files & Permissions
49	
50	All Archon state files are in `.archon/`. The table below covers **phase agents** (plan, prover, review) and the **user**. Subagent permissions are NOT a fixed row — they are descriptor-driven (`write_domain` glob in the subagent's frontmatter, enforced at dispatch time). See "Subagent permissions" after the table.
51	
52	| File | Plan | Prover | Review | User |
53	|------|------|--------|--------|------|
54	| `PROGRESS.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
55	| `STRATEGY.md` | read + write | — | — | read |
56	| `USER_HINTS.md` | **loop-managed** (captured + cleared by the CLI; injected into the plan prompt) | — | — | write |
57	| `task_pending.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
58	| `task_done.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
59	| `task_results/<file>.md` | read (collect) | write (own file) | read only | read |
60	| `proof-journal/` | read | — | **write** | read |
61	| `PROJECT_STATUS.md` | read | — | **write** (Knowledge Base only — session log lives in `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`) | read |
62	| `iter/iter-NNN/plan.md` | **write** (this iter only) | — | read (recent window injected) | read |
63	| `iter/iter-NNN/review.md` | read (recent window injected) | — | **write** (this iter only) | read |
64	| `iter/iter-NNN/objectives.md` | optional write | — | read | read |
65	| `archon-protected.yaml` | read | read | read | **write** |
66	| `.lean` files | — | write (own file; frozen protected signatures) | — | write (via comments) |
67	| `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex` | **write** (informal prose, `\lean{...}` hints) | read | **write** (semantic markers: `\mathlibok`, `% NOTE:`, `\lean{...}` corrections — NOT `\leanok`) | read |
68	| `analogies/<slug>.md` | read (when relevant) | — | — | read |
69	| `TO_USER.md` | **write** (shared notice board) | **write** (shared notice board) | **write** (shared notice board) | read |
70	
71	### Subagent permissions
72	
73	Each subagent ships YAML frontmatter:
74	
75	- `write_domain: "<glob>"` — file pattern the subagent may write. Empty for purely read-only subagents (the dispatching call typically passes `--write-domain 'task_results/**'` so the subagent can still write its own report).
76	- `read_only: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may only write its `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`.
77	- `can_spawn: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may dispatch children whose `write_domain` is a subset of its own.
78	
79	The dispatcher enforces `write_domain` at the filesystem level: any child whose declared domain is not a subset of the parent's is rejected before the child agent starts. Reads are restricted only by the descriptor's prompt body (e.g. fresh-context critics that are told not to read PROGRESS.md / STRATEGY.md).
80	
81	**`.archon/REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md`** is reserved for the interactive `archon refactor draft` / `archon refactor run` flow run by hand by the mathematician. The autonomous loop never reads or writes that file. To invoke a refactor-style subagent inside the loop, write the directive to a fresh tempfile under `.archon/logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-directive.md` and dispatch via the generic wrapper. Older state files (`STRATEGY.md`, `task_pending.md`, `PROGRESS.md`) may contain leftover references to the old REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md flow — treat those as historical noise.
82	
83	## Protected Declarations
84	
85	`archon-protected.yaml` at the project root lists declarations whose **signatures are frozen** by the mathematician. Rules:
86	
87	- **Plan / prover / review** agents: read-only on protected signatures. May fill proof bodies; may not rename, re-type, or reorder arguments.
88	- **Write-capable structural subagents** (per their `write_domain`): may *move* a protected declaration to a different file (name + signature preserved verbatim) and must then update the path key in `archon-protected.yaml`. They may NEVER rename / re-type / delete / re-sign a protected declaration.
89	- The YAML file itself is user-only. No agent adds or removes a declaration. The only allowed agent edit is updating an existing declaration's file path.
90	
91	## Blueprint Marker Vocabulary
92	
93	Two semantic markers:
94	
95	- `\leanok` — statement block: declaration is formalized (at least a `sorry` present). Proof block: proof closed, no `sorry`.
96	- `\mathlibok` — statement block: declaration is backed by Mathlib (re-export / alias); no Archon proof obligation remains.
97	
98	No marker means the block is unformalized. If a block fails to translate, leave it unmarked and annotate with a `% NOTE:` comment.
99	
100	**`\leanok` is managed deterministically by the `sync_leanok` phase** that runs between prover and review each iteration. It walks every chapter, looks up each `\lean{...}` declaration, runs `sorry_analyzer` + `lake env lean`, and adds/removes `\leanok` accordingly. Agents must NOT add or remove `\leanok` themselves.
101	
102	`\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, and `% NOTE:` annotations are the review agent's domain (they require semantic judgement). The plan agent writes informal prose and `\lean{...}` hints; provers never touch the blueprint.
103	
104	## User Interaction
105	
106	The user provides hints in two places:
107	
108	- **Strategic hints** → `.archon/USER_HINTS.md`. The Archon loop captures this file's content before each plan phase, injects it into the plan agent's prompt, and resets the file to its bundled template (an HTML-comment format guide + zero bullets) after the plan phase succeeds. The HTML-comment preamble is stripped before injection so a template-only file renders as "no hints" to the planner. The plan agent does NOT read or clear that file itself. Provers never see these hints.
109	- **File-specific hints** → `/- USER: ... -/` comments directly in `.lean` files. The prover that owns that file sees them naturally.
110	
111	Archon talks back to the user through **`TO_USER.md`** — a single, *persistent* notice board surfaced as a UI banner. It is **shared and writable by every agent** (plan, prover, review). It is no longer cleared at the start of each review phase; content survives across iters until an agent prunes it. The discipline every writer follows:
112	
113	- **Concise**: the whole file stays ≤ 2–3 short bullets. It is a banner, not a log.
114	- **Relevant now**: before adding anything, read the current content and **delete any bullet that is no longer true at this point** (the blocker was resolved, the decision was overtaken, the credential got set). Never let it accumulate stale notices.
115	- **Notice board, never a question queue**: the loop is autonomous and may run unattended for many iters. Never write "awaiting your decision", an options menu, a "where to reply", or "default to X if no reply" — anything implying the loop is paused for a human. State decisions as *made* and blockers as *worked-around-as-far-as-possible*; the user steers asynchronously via `USER_HINTS.md`.
116	
117	## Agent Roles
118	
119	### Plan Agent
120	
121	- Read `.archon/prompts/plan.md` for full instructions.
122	- User hints, blueprint-doctor findings, recent iter sidecars, the subagent catalog, and the references summary are all pre-injected into your invocation prompt. You do not "go read" those files.
123	- Read `task_results/` to collect prover and subagent results, then update `task_pending.md` / `task_done.md`.
124	- Optionally invoke subagents (via the auto-injected catalog) before writing prover objectives. Subagent reports are auto-archived to `logs/iter-NNN/` by the Archon CLI.
125	- Write `PROGRESS.md` with objectives for the next prover round.
126	- Write informal prose in `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex`. Do NOT touch markers — `\leanok` is owned by the deterministic sync; `\mathlibok` / `% NOTE:` by the review agent.
127	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or fill sorries yourself.
128	
129	### Prover Agent
130	
131	- Read `PROGRESS.md` for your objectives (read only).
132	- Your **active mode** is injected directly into your invocation prompt — read it carefully, it defines your goal, workflow, and constraints for this session.
133	- When your mode specifies `read_blueprint: true`, you are permitted to **read** your assigned blueprint chapter (`blueprint/src/chapters/<your_slug>.tex`) for the informal proof sketch. You may never write to blueprint chapters.
134	- Write results to `task_results/<your_file>.md`.
135	- Write only to the `.lean` file(s) you are assigned — never edit another agent's file.
136	- Check for `/- USER: ... -/` comments in your file for file-specific hints.
137	- Do NOT edit blueprint chapters. Flag in your task result which declarations are ready for which marker; the review agent applies them.
138	
139	### Subagents
140	
141	Subagents are descriptor-driven and discovered at runtime. The catalog is auto-injected into the plan and review prompts each phase (see "Subagents" above for the wrapper invocation). Each subagent reads only what its directive points at — never `PROGRESS.md` / `STRATEGY.md` / phase-agent state — and writes a self-contained report at `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`. Descriptors with `can_spawn: true` may dispatch children (subject to the global `loop.max_parallel` cap).
142	
143	### Review Agent
144	
145	- Read `.archon/prompts/review.md` for full instructions.
146	- Read `proof-journal/current_session/attempts_raw.jsonl` for structured prover attempt data.
147	- Write the session journal to `proof-journal/sessions/session_N/` (`summary.md`, `milestones.jsonl`, `recommendations.md`).
148	- Update `PROJECT_STATUS.md` (Knowledge Base only — session narrative goes to `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`).
149	- Maintain the semantic blueprint markers — `\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, `% NOTE:` annotations, stale `\notready` removal. Do NOT touch `\leanok`.
150	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or modify `PROGRESS.md`.
151	
152	### Loop infrastructure (no agent role)
153	
154	These run automatically between phases:
155	
156	- **Iter sidecar init** — creates `.archon/iter/iter-NNN/` at iter start so plan + review have a stable destination for per-iter narrative. Top-level files (STRATEGY.md, PROJECT_STATUS.md, task_*.md) stay bounded.
157	- **`sync_leanok`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically updating `\leanok` markers based on actual sorry counts and compilation status.
158	- **`blueprint-doctor`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically detecting orphan chapters, broken `\ref` / `\uses` / `\proves`, and new `axiom` declarations. Findings are written to `logs/iter-NNN/blueprint-doctor.{md,json}` and inlined into the next plan agent's prompt.

36	  supplement below): the categorical bridge step. For an injective object `I` of an abelian
37	  category, the contravariant `Hom(-, I) = preadditiveYoneda.obj I` is exact (preserves
38	  finite colimits, hence homology), so it carries a quasi-isomorphism of chain complexes to

61	/-
62	Planner strategy for `cechComplex_hom_identification`:
63	────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
64	
65	Goal: construct a cochain-complex isomorphism
66	  `Hom(K(𝒰)_•, F) ≅ Č•(𝒰, F)`
67	where:
68	  • `K(𝒰)_p = ∐_{σ : Fin(p+1) → 𝒰.I₀} freeYoneda.obj (coverInterOpen 𝒰 σ)`
69	    (the degree-`p` term of `cechFreePresheafComplex 𝒰`)
70	  • `Č^p(𝒰, F) = ∏_{σ : Fin(p+1) → 𝒰.I₀} F.obj (coverInterOpen 𝒰 σ)`
71	    (the degree-`p` term of `sectionCechComplex 𝒰 F`)
72	  Both complexes live in `Ab` after applying the global-sections functor.
73	
74	Step 1 — per-degree hom-coproduct duality:
75	  For each `p`, the Yoneda/free-presheaf adjunction gives
76	    `Hom(freeYoneda.obj V, F) ≅ F.obj V`  (natural in `V`).
77	  This is exactly `AlgebraicGeometry.freeYonedaHomAddEquiv` from `PresheafCech.lean`.
78	  Combined with the coproduct-hom identity
79	    `Hom(∐_σ A_σ, F) ≅ ∏_σ Hom(A_σ, F)`
80	  (via `preadditiveYoneda` preservation of limits, or hand-rolled from
81	  `Limits.Sigma.desc` / `Limits.Sigma.ι`), we get a degree-wise `Ab`-iso
82	    `Hom(K(𝒰)_p, F) ≅ ∏_σ F.obj (coverInterOpen 𝒰 σ) = Č^p(𝒰, F)`.
83	
84	Step 2 — differential intertwining:
85	  Show that the degree-wise isos commute with the differentials.  The differential on
86	  `Hom(K(𝒰)_•, F)` is precomposition with the Čech boundary `d_p : K(𝒰)_{p+1} → K(𝒰)_p`
87	  (the alternating sum of face maps of `cechFreeSimplicial`).  The differential on
88	  `Č•(𝒰, F)` is the alternating sum of restriction maps built by `sectionCechComplex`.
89	  These match under the per-degree iso by naturality of `freeYonedaHomAddEquiv`.
90	
91	Step 3 — assemble:
92	  Use `HomologicalComplex.Hom.isoOfComponents` (or equivalent) to combine the per-degree
93	  isos into a full cochain-complex iso, supplying the naturality squares from Step 2.
94	
95	Key API:
96	  • `AlgebraicGeometry.freeYonedaHomAddEquiv` (PresheafCech.lean): the per-degree iso.
97	  • `Limits.Sigma.desc`, `Limits.Sigma.ι`: coproduct universal property in
98	    `X.PresheafOfModules`.
99	  • `HomologicalComplex.Hom.isoOfComponents`: assemble component-wise isos into a
100	    full complex iso.
101	  • `sectionCechComplex` (PresheafCech.lean): the target complex.
102	  • `cechFreePresheafComplex` / `cechFreePresheafComplex_X` (FreePresheafComplex.lean):
103	    the source complex and its degreewise unfolding lemma.
104	-/

106	/-! ## Project-local Mathlib supplement — Čech hom-identification -/
107	
108	variable {X : Scheme.{u}}
109	
110	/-- **The hom cosimplicial abelian group `Hom(K(𝒰)_•, F)`.**
111	
112	The cosimplicial object in `Ab` obtained by applying the contravariant additive functor
113	`Hom_{PMod}(-, F) = preadditiveYoneda.obj F` to the free Čech simplicial object
114	`cechFreeSimplicial 𝒰`.  Its value in degree `p` is the hom-group
115	`Hom_{PMod}(K(𝒰)_p, F)`, and its alternating-coface-map cochain complex is the left-hand
116	side of the Čech hom-identification.  Defining it this way makes the differential of the
117	hom-complex literally the alternating sum of `Hom(faceᵢ, F)`, so the identification with
118	`sectionCechComplex` reduces to a cosimplicial natural isomorphism.
119	
120	Project-local: Mathlib has neither `cechFreeSimplicial` nor this contravariant transport. -/
121	noncomputable def homCechCosimplicial (𝒰 : X.OpenCover) [Finite 𝒰.I₀]
122	    (F : X.PresheafOfModules) : CosimplicialObject Ab.{u} :=
123	  (cechFreeSimplicial 𝒰).rightOp ⋙ preadditiveYoneda.obj F
124	
125	/-- **The hom cochain complex `Hom(K(𝒰)_•, F)`** — the left-hand side of the Čech
126	hom-identification.
127	
128	The alternating-coface-map cochain complex of `homCechCosimplicial 𝒰 F`. Built the same way as
129	`sectionCechComplex` (PresheafCech.lean) so that the planned identification
130	`cechComplex_hom_identification : homCechComplex 𝒰 F ≅ sectionCechComplex (coverOpen 𝒰) F` is just
131	`(alternatingCofaceMapComplex Ab).mapIso` of the cosimplicial natural isomorphism.
132	
133	Project-local: the hom-complex of the free Čech resolution has no Mathlib counterpart. -/
134	noncomputable def homCechComplex (𝒰 : X.OpenCover) [Finite 𝒰.I₀]
135	    (F : X.PresheafOfModules) : CochainComplex Ab.{u} ℕ :=
136	  (AlgebraicTopology.alternatingCofaceMapComplex Ab.{u}).obj (homCechCosimplicial 𝒰 F)
137	
138	/-- **Per-degree component of the Čech hom-identification.**
139	
140	For each simplicial degree `n`, the abelian-group isomorphism
141	`Hom_{PMod}(K(𝒰)_n, F) ≅ ∏_σ F(⨅ₖ U(σ k))` assembled from (i) the coproduct–hom duality
142	`Hom(∐_σ Aσ, F) ≅ ∏_σ Hom(Aσ, F)` (the additive functor `Hom(-, F)` preserves the limit,
143	so `piComparison` is an iso, combined with `opCoproductIsoProduct`), and (ii) the per-index
144	free–Yoneda section identification `freeYonedaHomAddEquiv`.
145	
146	Project-local: this is the degreewise core of `lem:cech_complex_hom_identification`. -/
147	noncomputable def homCechSectionIsoApp (𝒰 : X.OpenCover) [Finite 𝒰.I₀]
148	    (F : X.PresheafOfModules) (n : SimplexCategory) :
149	    (homCechCosimplicial 𝒰 F).obj n ≅ (sectionCechCosimplicial (coverOpen 𝒰) F).obj n :=
150	  (preadditiveYoneda.obj F).mapIso
151	      (opCoproductIsoProduct
152	        (fun σ : Fin (n.len + 1) → 𝒰.I₀ => freeYoneda.obj (coverInterOpen 𝒰 σ)))
153	    ≪≫ asIso (piComparison (preadditiveYoneda.obj F)
154	        (fun σ : Fin (n.len + 1) → 𝒰.I₀ => Opposite.op (freeYoneda.obj (coverInterOpen 𝒰 σ))))
155	    ≪≫ Limits.Pi.mapIso (fun σ : Fin (n.len + 1) → 𝒰.I₀ =>
156	        (freeYonedaHomAddEquiv (coverInterOpen 𝒰 σ) F).toAddCommGrpIso)
157	
158	/-- `Pi.mapIso e` is by definition `Pi.map (fun b => (e b).hom)`. -/
159	private lemma pi_mapIso_hom_eq {β : Type*} {C : Type*} [Category C] {f g : β → C}
160	    [HasProductsOfShape β C] (e : ∀ b, f b ≅ g b) :
161	    (Limits.Pi.mapIso e).hom = Limits.Pi.map (fun b => (e b).hom) := rfl
162	
163	/-- Characterizing property of `homCechSectionIsoApp`: its `σ`-component is precomposition
164	with the coproduct injection `Sigma.ι σ` followed by the free–Yoneda section identification.
165	Reduces the naturality of the assembled iso to the naturality of `freeYonedaHomAddEquiv`. -/
166	private lemma homCechSectionIsoApp_hom_π (𝒰 : X.OpenCover) [Finite 𝒰.I₀]
167	    (F : X.PresheafOfModules) (n : SimplexCategory) (σ : Fin (n.len + 1) → 𝒰.I₀) :
168	    (homCechSectionIsoApp 𝒰 F n).hom ≫
169	        Pi.π (fun τ : Fin (n.len + 1) → 𝒰.I₀ =>
170	          F.presheaf.obj (Opposite.op (coverInterOpen 𝒰 τ))) σ =
171	      (preadditiveYoneda.obj F).map
172	          (Limits.Sigma.ι (fun τ : Fin (n.len + 1) → 𝒰.I₀ =>
173	            freeYoneda.obj (coverInterOpen 𝒰 τ)) σ).op ≫
174	        (freeYonedaHomAddEquiv (coverInterOpen 𝒰 σ) F).toAddCommGrpIso.hom := by
175	  rw [homCechSectionIsoApp, Iso.trans_hom, Iso.trans_hom, Functor.mapIso_hom, asIso_hom,
176	    pi_mapIso_hom_eq]
177	  simp only [Category.assoc]
178	  erw [Pi.map_π, piComparison_comp_π_assoc]
179	  rw [← Category.assoc]
180	  congr 1
181	  exact ((preadditiveYoneda.obj F).map_comp _ _).symm.trans
182	    (congrArg _ (opCoproductIsoProduct_hom_comp_π σ))
183	
184	/-- **Naturality of the free–Yoneda section identification in the open.**
185	
186	For an inclusion of opens `h : V ⟶ W`, precomposition with `freeYoneda.map h` on the hom-side
187	corresponds to restriction `F.presheaf.map h.op` on the section side.  This is the single
188	naturality square that powers the cosimplicial naturality of the Čech hom-identification. -/
189	private lemma freeYonedaHomAddEquiv_naturality {V W : TopologicalSpace.Opens ↥X}
190	    (h : V ⟶ W) (F : X.PresheafOfModules) :
191	    (preadditiveYoneda.obj F).map (freeYoneda.map h).op ≫
192	        (freeYonedaHomAddEquiv V F).toAddCommGrpIso.hom
193	      = (freeYonedaHomAddEquiv W F).toAddCommGrpIso.hom ≫ F.presheaf.map h.op := by
194	  ext ψ
195	  show freeYonedaHomEquiv V F (freeYoneda.map h ≫ ψ)
196	      = (ConcreteCategory.hom (F.presheaf.map h.op)) (freeYonedaHomEquiv W F ψ)
197	  show yonedaEquiv (PresheafOfModules.freeHomEquiv (freeYoneda.map h ≫ ψ))
198	      = (ConcreteCategory.hom (F.presheaf.map h.op))
199	          (yonedaEquiv (PresheafOfModules.freeHomEquiv ψ))
200	  rw [show PresheafOfModules.freeHomEquiv (freeYoneda.map h ≫ ψ)
201	        = yoneda.map h ≫ PresheafOfModules.freeHomEquiv ψ from
202	      (PresheafOfModules.freeAdjunction _).homEquiv_naturality_left (yoneda.map h) ψ,
203	    ← yonedaEquiv_naturality]
204	  rfl
205	
206	/-- **The cosimplicial natural isomorphism `Hom(K(𝒰)_•, F) ≅ Č•(𝒰, F)`.**
207	
208	Assembles the per-degree isomorphisms `homCechSectionIsoApp` into a natural isomorphism of
209	cosimplicial abelian groups. The naturality square reduces, via `homCechSectionIsoApp_hom_π`
210	in both degrees, to the single naturality square `freeYonedaHomAddEquiv_naturality` of the
211	free–Yoneda section identification.
212	
213	Project-local: the cosimplicial comparison of the hom-complex with the section complex has no
214	Mathlib counterpart. -/
215	noncomputable def homCechSectionCosimplicialIso (𝒰 : X.OpenCover) [Finite 𝒰.I₀]
216	    (F : X.PresheafOfModules) :
217	    homCechCosimplicial 𝒰 F ≅ sectionCechCosimplicial (coverOpen 𝒰) F :=
218	  NatIso.ofComponents (homCechSectionIsoApp 𝒰 F) (by
219	    intro n m f
220	    apply Limits.Pi.hom_ext
221	    intro σ
222	    dsimp only [sectionCechCosimplicial]
223	    rw [Category.assoc, Category.assoc, Pi.lift_π]
224	    erw [homCechSectionIsoApp_hom_π 𝒰 F m σ]
225	    conv_rhs => rw [← Category.assoc]
226	    erw [homCechSectionIsoApp_hom_π 𝒰 F n (σ ∘ ⇑(SimplexCategory.Hom.toOrderHom f))]
227	    erw [Category.assoc]
228	    erw [← freeYonedaHomAddEquiv_naturality
229	        (homOfLE (coverInterOpen_comp_le 𝒰 ⇑(SimplexCategory.Hom.toOrderHom f) σ)) F]
230	    dsimp only [homCechCosimplicial, Functor.comp_map, Functor.rightOp_map]
231	    erw [← Category.assoc, ← Functor.map_comp, ← Category.assoc, ← Functor.map_comp]
232	    congr 1
233	    congr 1
234	    apply Quiver.Hom.unop_inj
235	    simp only [unop_comp]
236	    dsimp only [cechFreeSimplicial]
237	    erw [Limits.Sigma.ι_desc]
238	    rfl
239	    )
240	
241	/-- **The Čech hom-identification** (blueprint `lem:cech_complex_hom_identification`).
242	
243	The cochain-complex isomorphism `Hom_{PMod}(K(𝒰)_•, F) ≅ Č•(𝒰, F)` identifying the
244	hom-complex of the free Čech resolution with the section Čech complex of `F`. Obtained by
245	applying the alternating-coface-map complex functor to the cosimplicial natural isomorphism
246	`homCechSectionCosimplicialIso`, so the differential intertwining is automatic from the
247	cosimplicial naturality.
248	
249	Project-local: the comparison of the free-resolution hom-complex with the section complex has
250	no Mathlib counterpart. -/

266	/-! ## Project-local Mathlib supplement — `Hom(-, I)` is exact for injective `I`
267	
268	The presheaf-level Čech-acyclicity argument (`lem:injective_cech_acyclic`) needs the single
269	categorical fact that, for an injective object `I` of an abelian category, the contravariant
270	hom functor `Hom(-, I)` is exact — equivalently, the covariant functor
271	`preadditiveYoneda.obj I : Cᵒᵖ ⥤ Ab` preserves homology. Mathlib supplies the two pieces:
272	`preadditiveYoneda.obj I` always preserves finite limits (it is left exact), and injectivity
273	is equivalent to it preserving epimorphisms; we assemble these into preservation of finite
274	colimits (hence of homology), and feed the result through
275	`HomologicalComplex.quasiIsoAt_map_of_preservesHomology` to obtain quasi-isomorphism
276	preservation. These are stated for a general abelian category so they apply verbatim to
277	`X.PresheafOfModules` once the injectivity of `I` as a presheaf of modules is in hand.
278	
279	Project-local: Mathlib has the ingredients but not the packaged exactness of `Hom(-, I)` as a
280	homology-preserving functor. -/
281	
282	section InjectiveHomExact
283	
284	open CategoryTheory Limits
285	
286	variable {C : Type*} [Category C] [Abelian C]
287	
288	/-- **`Hom(-, I)` preserves finite colimits for injective `I`.** For an injective object `I`
289	of an abelian category, the covariant functor `preadditiveYoneda.obj I : Cᵒᵖ ⥤ Ab`
290	preserves finite colimits: it is left exact (preserves finite limits) automatically, and
291	injectivity makes it preserve epimorphisms, which together with left exactness gives
292	exactness. Combined with the automatic `PreservesFiniteLimits` instance this yields
293	`(preadditiveYoneda.obj I).PreservesHomology` by `Functor.preservesHomologyOfExact`.
294	
295	Project-local: the exactness of `Hom(-, I)` for injective `I` is the categorical engine of
296	`lem:injective_cech_acyclic`. -/
297	instance preadditiveYoneda_obj_preservesFiniteColimits_of_injective
298	    (I : C) [Injective I] : PreservesFiniteColimits (preadditiveYoneda.obj I) := by
299	  have hepi : (preadditiveYoneda.obj I).PreservesEpimorphisms :=
300	    (Injective.injective_iff_preservesEpimorphisms_preadditiveYoneda_obj I).mp inferInstance
301	  rw [Functor.preservesFiniteColimits_iff_forall_exact_map_and_epi]
302	  intro S hS
303	  have hg := hS.epi_g
304	  exact ⟨hS.exact.map_of_mono_of_preservesKernel _ hS.mono_f inferInstance,
305	    hepi.preserves S.g⟩
306	
307	/-- **`Hom(-, I)` carries quasi-isomorphisms to quasi-isomorphisms for injective `I`.**
308	Since `preadditiveYoneda.obj I` preserves homology
309	(`preadditiveYoneda_obj_preservesFiniteColimits_of_injective`), applying it degreewise to a
310	quasi-isomorphism `φ` of chain complexes in `Cᵒᵖ` produces a quasi-isomorphism of the
311	`Hom`-cochain complexes. This is the form consumed by `injective_cech_acyclic`: the
312	augmentation `cechFreeComplexAug 𝒰` (a quasi-isomorphism once Lane 1 lands), taken opposite
313	and mapped through `preadditiveYoneda.obj F`, stays a quasi-isomorphism.
314	
315	Project-local: packages `quasiIsoAt_map_of_preservesHomology` against the injective-hom
316	exactness instance above. -/
317	lemma quasiIso_map_preadditiveYoneda_of_injective (I : C) [Injective I]
318	    {ι : Type*} {c : ComplexShape ι} {K L : HomologicalComplex Cᵒᵖ c}
319	    (φ : K ⟶ L) [QuasiIso φ] :
320	    QuasiIso (((preadditiveYoneda.obj I).mapHomologicalComplex c).map φ) := by
321	  rw [quasiIso_iff]
322	  intro i
323	  exact HomologicalComplex.quasiIsoAt_map_of_preservesHomology φ (preadditiveYoneda.obj I) i
324	    (hφ := QuasiIso.quasiIsoAt (f := φ) i)
325	
326	end InjectiveHomExact
327	
328	end AlgebraicGeometry
